schools.summary: Moses speaking the law of vows to the heads of the tribes: 'and Moses spake unto the heads of the tribes concerning the children of Israel, saying, This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded' — Moses delivered the law of vows to the leaders of the tribes, as God commanded (the Moses spake unto the heads of the tribes, the law given to the leaders, who would teach the people, the This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded, the divine authority of the law, the figure, the law of vows committed first to the heads of the tribes, that order be kept in its teaching, the truth that God's commands come with his authority, 'this is the thing which the LORD hath commanded,' the figure of the orderly delivery of God's law through the appointed leaders, the law of vows, touching the binding of the word and the authority in the household, given by God); the law given to the heads of the tribes (the leaders taught) — the delivery (to the leaders); 'This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded' (the divine authority of the law, God's command, the figure, the law given with God's authority, the providence, the law introduced, the binding of vows to follow, vv.2) — Henry, Gill.

schools.summary: The binding of a man's vow and the sanctity of his word: 'if a man vow a vow unto the LORD, or swear an oath to bind his soul with a bond; he shall not break his word, he shall do according to all that proceedeth out of his mouth' — the binding word — 'if a man vow a vow unto the LORD, or swear an oath to bind his soul with a bond; he shall not break his word, he shall do according to all that proceedeth out of his mouth': a man who vows or swears to God is bound to perform it and must not break his word (the witnesses noting the significance — the inviolable obligation of the vow, the man bound by his own word, the oath that binds the soul, the solemn duty to perform what is promised to God, the lesson — the sanctity of the word and the duty of keeping vows, the truth that what is freely vowed becomes a binding obligation, 'when thou vowest a vow unto God, defer not to pay it... that which thou hast vowed... pay,' Ecclesiastes 5:4-5, 'better is it that thou shouldest not vow, than that thou shouldest vow and not pay,' the binding of the soul, the word once given to God not to be broken, the figure — the faithfulness that keeps its word, 'he that sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not,' Psalm 15:4, the truth that God himself is faithful to his word, and his people are to be faithful to theirs, the warning against the rash or broken vow, the obligation to perform all that proceeds out of the mouth, the figure of the sanctity of the word and the binding power of the vow made to God, the figure of the inviolable bond of the word vowed to the LORD); the man binding his soul by vow or oath to God (the solemn obligation, the bond on the soul) — the vow (the binding bond); the duty to perform the vow, not breaking the word (the sanctity of the word, Ecclesiastes 5:4-5; Psalm 15:4) — the figure (the faithfulness that keeps its word, the providence, the law of the man's vow, the vows of women to follow, vv.3-15) — Calvin, Keil, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does the law of the binding vow establish?

sides.position: The inviolable obligation of the vow that binds the soul, the duty to perform all that is promised to God and not break one's word - the sanctity of the word once given (Ecclesiastes 5:4-5), a figure of the faithfulness that keeps its word even to its own hurt (Psalm 15:4), answering to the faithfulness of God who keeps his word

schools.summary: The case of a young woman's vow in her father's house: 'if a woman also vow a vow unto the LORD, and bind herself by a bond, being in her father's house in her youth' — the law now turns to the vow of a young unmarried woman still under her father (the if a woman also vow a vow unto the LORD, the woman's vow, the and bind herself by a bond, the obligation taken, the being in her father's house in her youth, the woman still under her father's authority, the figure, the vow of the young woman, made while under the authority of her father, the truth that the woman's vow, unlike the man's, was subject to the authority over her, the father's headship, the figure of the order of the household, the authority that may confirm or annul the vow, vv.4-5, the wisdom of God's law that the rash vow of the young be subject to the elder's judgment, lest she be ensnared); the young woman's vow and bond (the obligation) — the vow (the woman's bond); 'being in her father's house in her youth' (the woman under her father, the authority over her, the figure, the vow subject to the father's judgment, the providence, the case, the father's confirming or disallowing to follow, vv.4-5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The father's silence confirming the daughter's vow: 'and her father hear her vow, and her bond wherewith she hath bound her soul, and her father shall hold his peace at her: then all her vows shall stand, and every bond wherewith she hath bound her soul shall stand' — if the father, hearing the vow, said nothing, his silence confirmed it, and the vow stood (the her father hear her vow, the vow heard, the her father shall hold his peace at her, the silence, the tacit consent, the then all her vows shall stand, the vow confirmed by silence, the figure, the father's silence taken as consent, the vow standing, the truth that the authority who hears and does not forbid is held to allow, the figure of the tacit confirmation, the vow binding where the father consents, the order that the vow, once allowed, is binding as the man's, vv.2, the wisdom that the silence of the head confirms the bond); the father hearing the vow (the vow known) — the hearing (the vow heard); 'her father shall hold his peace at her: then all her vows shall stand' (the silence confirming the vow, the tacit consent, the figure, the vow binding by the father's consent, the providence, the confirmation, the father's disallowing to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The father's power to annul the daughter's vow and the LORD's forgiving her: 'but if her father disallow her in the day that he heareth; not any of her vows, or of her bonds wherewith she hath bound her soul, shall stand: and the LORD shall forgive her, because her father disallowed her' — the annulled vow and the forgiveness — 'if her father disallow her in the day that he heareth; not any of her vows... shall stand: and the LORD shall forgive her, because her father disallowed her': if the father, on the day he heard, forbade the vow, it did not stand, and God forgave her the non-performance (the witnesses noting the significance — the authority of the father to annul the vow, and God's gracious release of the daughter from the bond, the father's headship overriding the vow, and the LORD not holding her guilty for what was not in her power to perform, the lesson — the gentleness of God's law, that the one under authority is not bound by a vow her head forbids, and is forgiven the breaking, the forgiveness 'because her father disallowed her,' the release granted by God himself, the truth that obligation rests on what is in one's power, and God does not require what authority has annulled, the figure — the grace that forgives where the bond is lawfully released, the tender provision for the one under another's authority, the figure of the headship that may release from a rash vow, and the God who forgives the released, 'the LORD shall forgive her,' the truth that God's law tempers the binding of vows with the order of the household and the grace of forgiveness, the figure of the gracious release from the vow by the authority God appointed, and the forgiveness God grants, the figure of the authority that releases and the LORD who forgives); the father annulling the vow on the day he heard, the bond not standing (the headship that releases) — the annulling (the vow voided); the God forgiving the daughter the non-performance, since her father disallowed her (the grace that releases, the obligation resting on what is in one's power) — the figure (the God who forgives the lawfully released, the providence, the release, the married woman's vow to follow, vv.6-8) — Calvin, Ellicott, JFB, Wesley, Gill.

schools.summary: The husband annulling the wife's vow and the LORD forgiving her: 'but if her husband disallowed her on the day that he heard it; then he shall make her vow which she vowed, and that which she uttered with her lips, wherewith she bound her soul, of none effect: and the LORD shall forgive her' — if the husband forbade the vow on the day he heard, it was made void, and God forgave her (the if her husband disallowed her on the day that he heard it, the husband forbidding, the he shall make her vow... of none effect, the vow annulled, the and the LORD shall forgive her, the forgiveness, the release, the figure, the husband's authority annulling the vow, and God's gracious release of the wife, the truth that the wife is not bound by a vow her husband forbids, and is forgiven the non-performance, the same gentleness as for the father, vv.5, the figure of the headship that releases and the God who forgives, the order of the household and the grace of forgiveness); the husband annulling the vow (the headship that releases) — the annulling (the vow voided); 'the LORD shall forgive her' (God forgiving the wife the non-performance, the grace that releases, the figure, the God who forgives the lawfully released, the providence, the release, the widow's vow to follow, vv.9) — Gill.

schools.summary: The vow of a widow or divorced woman standing: 'but every vow of a widow, and of her that is divorced, wherewith they have bound their souls, shall stand against her' — the widow or divorced woman, having no husband over her, was bound by her vow, which stood (the every vow of a widow, and of her that is divorced, the woman with no head over her, the wherewith they have bound their souls, the obligation taken, the shall stand against her, the vow binding, as the man's, vv.2, the figure, the vow of the woman who is her own, under no father or husband, binding as the man's, the truth that where there is no authority to annul, the vow stands, the figure of the obligation that rests fully on the one who is free, the widow and divorced bound by their own word, the order that the vow is binding where there is none to forbid); the widow or divorced woman, under no head (the woman who is her own) — the case (no authority over her); 'shall stand against her' (the vow binding, as the man's, the obligation full, the figure, the vow standing where there is none to annul, the providence, the binding vow, the wife's vow to afflict the soul to follow, vv.10-13) — Calvin, Gill.

schools.summary: The husband's silence confirming the wife's vow: 'and her husband heard it, and held his peace at her, and disallowed her not: then all her vows shall stand, and every bond wherewith she bound her soul shall stand' — if the husband heard and did not forbid, the wife's vow stood (the her husband heard it, and held his peace at her, the silence, the and disallowed her not, no prohibition, the then all her vows shall stand, the vow confirmed, the figure, the husband's silence and non-prohibition confirming the vow, the truth that the head who hears and does not forbid is held to allow, the vow binding, the figure of the tacit confirmation, the wife's vow standing by the husband's consent, the order of the household, the vow binding where the husband allows); the husband's silence and non-prohibition (the tacit consent) — the silence (the consent); 'then all her vows shall stand' (the vow confirmed by silence, the bond binding, the figure, the vow standing by consent, the providence, the confirmation, the husband's annulling to follow, vv.12-13) — JFB, Gill.

schools.summary: The husband making the wife's vow void and the LORD forgiving her: 'but if her husband hath utterly made them void on the day he heard them; then whatsoever proceeded out of her lips concerning her vows, or concerning the bond of her soul, shall not stand: her husband hath made them void; and the LORD shall forgive her' — if the husband annulled the vow on the day he heard, it did not stand, and God forgave her (the if her husband hath utterly made them void on the day he heard them, the husband annulling, the whatsoever proceeded out of her lips... shall not stand, the vow void, the her husband hath made them void; and the LORD shall forgive her, the release and forgiveness, the figure, the husband's authority making the vow void, and God's gracious release of the wife, the truth that the wife is not bound by a vow her husband annuls, and is forgiven, the same gentleness as for the daughter, vv.5, the figure of the headship that releases and the God who forgives, the order of the household and the grace of forgiveness); the husband making the vow void (the headship that releases) — the annulling (the vow voided); 'the LORD shall forgive her' (God forgiving the wife the non-performance, the grace that releases, the figure, the God who forgives the lawfully released, the providence, the release, the rule of the husband's silence or annulling to follow, vv.13-15) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The husband's authority to establish or annul every vow of his wife: 'every vow, and every binding oath to afflict the soul, her husband may establish it, or her husband may make it void' — the headship — 'her husband may establish it, or her husband may make it void': the husband held the authority to confirm or annul every vow or self-affliction his wife had bound on herself (the witnesses noting the significance — the husband's headship over the wife's vows, the authority of the husband, as the head, to establish the vow by his consent or to make it void by his prohibition, the lesson — the order of the household under God, the wife's vows subject to the husband's judgment, especially vows 'to afflict the soul,' fastings and abstinences, that the husband might temper or release, the head responsible for the household, the wife's obligations not independent of him, the truth that God ordained order in the family, the husband over the wife, as Christ over the church, 'the husband is the head of the wife, even as Christ is the head of the church,' Ephesians 5:23, the figure — the headship that may confirm or release, a figure of the authority God appoints in the household, the wisdom of the law that the wife's vow be not a snare to the household but subject to the head, the figure of the order of the family, the husband's authority over the wife's vows, tempered by love and responsibility, the figure of the headship that establishes or releases the wife's vow); the vows and self-afflictions, fastings, abstinences, the wife had bound on herself (the binding oaths) — the vows (to afflict the soul); the husband's authority to confirm or annul every vow, the headship over the wife's vows (the order of the household, Ephesians 5:23) — the figure (the authority God appoints in the family, as Christ over the church, the providence, the rule, the consequence of the husband's silence or delay to follow, vv.14-15) — Calvin, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does the husband's power to establish or make void establish?
